title: Primary Immunodeficiency
objectives: Recognize the warning signs of primary immunodeficiency
Classify primary immunodeficiencies by immune system component affected
Apply a stepwise diagnostic approach to suspected immunodeficiency
Understand treatment principles including immunoglobulin replacement
Identify when to refer to clinical immunology

guidelineReferences.name: AAAAI/ACAAI Practice Parameter
guidelineReferences.citation: Bonilla FA, et al. Practice parameter for the diagnosis and management of primary immunodeficiency. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2015;136(5):1186-205
keyPoints: Antibody deficiencies are most common (~50% of PIDs)
Initial workup: CBC, quantitative immunoglobulins, vaccine titers
CVID is most common clinically significant PID in adults
Early diagnosis prevents irreversible organ damage

guidelineReferences.name: Jeffrey Modell Foundation: 10 Warning Signs
guidelineReferences.citation: Jeffrey Modell Foundation Medical Advisory Board. 10 Warning Signs of Primary Immunodeficiency

vignette.text:
A 28-year-old woman is referred to allergy/immunology for recurrent sinopulmonary infections. She reports 4-5 sinus infections per year requiring antibiotics for the past 6 years, 2 episodes of pneumonia in the past 3 years (one requiring hospitalization), and chronic productive cough. She also has a history of autoimmune thyroiditis and ITP (immune thrombocytopenia) that resolved after splenectomy 2 years ago.

Past Medical History: Recurrent sinusitis, 2 pneumonias, autoimmune thyroiditis, ITP (s/p splenectomy), chronic diarrhea

Family History: Brother died at age 3 from 'severe infection' (details unknown), mother has 'low antibodies'

Medications: Levothyroxine, albuterol PRN

Allergies: Penicillin (rash)

physicalExam.HEENT: Nasal turbinate hypertrophy, post-nasal drip, NO tonsillar tissue visible
physicalExam.pulmonary: Scattered rhonchi bilateral bases, no wheezing

imaging.chestCT: Bilateral lower lobe bronchiectasis, no active infiltrate

questions.stem: This patient has 4-5 sinus infections/year, 2 pneumonias in 3 years, and a family history of early death from infection.
questions.question: What are the Jeffrey Modell Foundation's 10 Warning Signs of Primary Immunodeficiency?
questions.hint: Consider patterns of infection frequency and severity.

options.text: ≥4 ear infections/year, ≥2 serious sinus infections/year, ≥2 months antibiotics with poor effect, ≥2 pneumonias/year, failure to thrive, recurrent deep abscesses, persistent thrush, need for IV antibiotics, ≥2 deep-seated infections, family history of PID

questions.explanation: Jeffrey Modell Foundation 10 Warning Signs: (1) ≥4 new ear infections/year, (2) ≥2 serious sinus infections/year, (3) ≥2 months on antibiotics with little effect, (4) ≥2 pneumonias within a year, (5) Failure to thrive, (6) Recurrent deep skin/organ abscesses, (7) Persistent thrush or fungal skin infections, (8) Need for IV antibiotics to clear infections, (9) ≥2 deep-seated infections (sepsis, meningitis, osteomyelitis), (10) Family history of PID. Having ≥2 signs warrants evaluation.

whyChain.why: How many warning signs does this patient have?
whyChain.answer: At least 4: (1) ≥2 serious sinus infections/year (she has 4-5), (2) ≥2 pneumonias (she has 2 in 3 years), (3) Family history of PID (brother died from infection, mother has 'low antibodies'), (4) Arguably prolonged antibiotic courses. This strongly suggests evaluation for PID.

Autoimmunity is increasingly recognized as a feature of PID, not just infections. Up to 25% of CVID patients have autoimmune manifestations (cytopenias, thyroiditis, IBD). Her ITP and thyroiditis fit this pattern.

questions.stem: You are categorizing types of primary immunodeficiency.
questions.question: How are primary immunodeficiencies classified by the immune component affected?
questions.hint: Consider B cells, T cells, phagocytes, and complement.

options.text: Antibody (B cell) deficiencies (~50%), Combined T and B cell deficiencies, Phagocyte defects, Complement deficiencies, Innate immunity defects

questions.explanation: PID classification: (1) Antibody deficiencies (most common, ~50%): CVID, XLA, IgA deficiency, specific antibody deficiency; (2) Combined immunodeficiencies: SCID, Wiskott-Aldrich, DiGeorge; (3) Phagocyte defects: CGD, LAD; (4) Complement deficiencies: C1-C9 deficiencies, MBL deficiency; (5) Innate immunity defects: TLR defects, IRAK4 deficiency. Over 450 distinct genetic disorders now identified.

whyChain.why: Why are antibody deficiencies most common?
whyChain.answer: B cells undergo complex development and selection processes with many potential genetic failure points. Also, partial antibody deficiency is compatible with survival to adulthood, so cases accumulate. SCID (combined deficiency) without treatment is fatal in infancy.

Antibody deficiency: encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcus, H. influenzae), sinopulmonary infections. T cell/combined: opportunistic infections (PCP, CMV), fungi, severe viral. Phagocyte: staphylococcal abscesses, fungal (Aspergillus). Complement: Neisseria (meningococcus, gonococcus).

questions.stem: This patient is 28 years old with low immunoglobulins and poor vaccine responses.
questions.question: What is the most common clinically significant primary immunodeficiency presenting in adulthood?
questions.hint: Consider the timing of presentation and laboratory findings.

options.text: Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID) - low IgG + low IgA and/or IgM + impaired vaccine responses, typically presents in 2nd-4th decades
SCID (presents in first months of life)

questions.explanation: CVID is the most common symptomatic primary immunodeficiency in adults. Diagnostic criteria: (1) Low IgG (usually <500 mg/dL) PLUS low IgA and/or IgM, (2) Impaired vaccine responses (especially to polysaccharide antigens), (3) Exclusion of secondary causes, (4) Age >4 years. Peak onset: 2nd-3rd decade. Characterized by recurrent sinopulmonary infections, bronchiectasis, autoimmunity, and increased lymphoma risk.

whyChain.why: Does this patient meet CVID criteria?
whyChain.answer: Yes: IgG 285 (low), IgA 18 (low), IgM 22 (low), poor pneumococcal vaccine response (2/23 protective), normal protein vaccine response (tetanus/diphtheria). Age 28, no secondary cause identified. She has classic CVID.

whyChain.why: What is the significance of absent tonsillar tissue?
whyChain.answer: Absent or hypoplastic tonsils/lymphoid tissue can be a clue to antibody deficiency - B cells are needed for germinal center formation in lymphoid tissue. This physical exam finding should prompt consideration of PID.

questions.stem: You are evaluating this patient for suspected immunodeficiency.
questions.question: What is the stepwise diagnostic approach to suspected primary immunodeficiency?
questions.hint: Consider initial screening vs advanced testing.

options.text: Step 1: Exclude secondary causes; Step 2: CBC, quantitative immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM), vaccine titers (protein and polysaccharide); Step 3: If abnormal, flow cytometry for lymphocyte subsets; Step 4: Genetic testing if indicated

questions.explanation: Stepwise approach: (1) Exclude secondary immunodeficiency (HIV, medications, protein-losing conditions, malignancy); (2) Initial screening: CBC with differential (lymphopenia?), quantitative IgG/IgA/IgM, specific antibody titers to protein vaccines (tetanus, diphtheria) and polysaccharide vaccines (pneumococcal); (3) If abnormal: flow cytometry (lymphocyte subsets, memory B cells), IgG subclasses; (4) Advanced: functional assays, complement studies, genetic testing. Early immunology referral recommended.

whyChain.why: Why test both protein and polysaccharide vaccine responses?
whyChain.answer: Protein vaccines (tetanus, diphtheria) are T-dependent - require T cell help for B cell response. Polysaccharide vaccines (pneumococcal 23-valent) are T-independent. Selective failure to respond to polysaccharides suggests specific antibody deficiency or CVID. This patient responds to protein but not polysaccharide - classic for CVID.

whyChain.why: What do the flow cytometry results show?
whyChain.answer: Her B cells are present (8%), ruling out X-linked agammaglobulinemia (absent B cells). However, switched memory B cells are LOW (1.2%, normal >2%). This is characteristic of CVID - B cells are present but cannot complete class-switching and maturation.

questions.stem: You are interpreting this patient's immunoglobulin levels.
questions.question: How do you interpret quantitative immunoglobulin levels?

options.text: IgE is the most important immunoglobulin
IgG <700 mg/dL is low; IgG <400-500 mg/dL strongly suggests antibody deficiency; must correlate with clinical history and vaccine responses; single low value should be confirmed
Immunoglobulin levels don't matter if patient has infections

questions.explanation: Interpretation: IgG <700 mg/dL is below normal; IgG <400-500 mg/dL strongly suggests clinically significant deficiency. CVID typically has IgG <500 + low IgA and/or IgM. Selective IgA deficiency: IgA <7 mg/dL with normal IgG/IgM. Important: (1) Correlate with clinical history; (2) Confirm with repeat testing; (3) Check vaccine responses even if immunoglobulins are low-normal; (4) Rule out secondary causes (protein loss, medications).

whyChain.why: Can someone have PID with normal immunoglobulin levels?
whyChain.answer: Yes - Specific Antibody Deficiency (SAD): normal quantitative immunoglobulins but impaired response to polysaccharide vaccines. Also, some complement and phagocyte defects have normal immunoglobulins. That's why vaccine titers are essential.

whyChain.why: What secondary causes should be excluded?
whyChain.answer: Secondary immunodeficiency causes: HIV, medications (rituximab, steroids, chemotherapy), protein-losing nephropathy/enteropathy, malignancy (multiple myeloma, CLL), post-HSCT. These are more common than PID in adults and must be ruled out.

questions.stem: This patient is diagnosed with CVID. You are discussing treatment.
questions.question: What is the mainstay treatment for primary antibody deficiencies?
questions.hint: Consider what replaces the missing immune component.

options.text: Bone marrow transplant for all patients
Immunoglobulin replacement therapy (IgG) - IV (IVIG) every 3-4 weeks or subcutaneous (SCIG) weekly/biweekly; goal trough IgG >500-800 mg/dL to reduce infections

questions.explanation: Immunoglobulin replacement therapy (IgRT) is the cornerstone of treatment for antibody deficiencies: (1) IVIG: IV infusion every 3-4 weeks, typically in infusion center; (2) SCIG: subcutaneous, self-administered at home weekly or biweekly; (3) Dose: 400-600 mg/kg/month, adjusted to maintain trough IgG >500-800 mg/dL; (4) Lifelong therapy required. Reduces infection frequency, hospitalizations, and antibiotic use. Does NOT cure the underlying defect.

whyChain.why: How do IVIG and SCIG compare?
whyChain.answer: Both equally effective. IVIG: larger volume, less frequent, requires IV access, infusion center. SCIG: smaller volume, more frequent, self-administered at home, more stable IgG levels, better QoL for many patients. Choice depends on patient preference and lifestyle.

Often used adjunctively, especially for patients with bronchiectasis or recurrent sinusitis. Common regimens: azithromycin 250-500mg 3x/week (also has anti-inflammatory effects). Does not replace IgRT but can reduce breakthrough infections.

questions.stem: The patient asks about prognosis and complications.
questions.question: What are the long-term complications of CVID and goals of therapy?

options.text: Complications: bronchiectasis (irreversible lung damage), autoimmunity, lymphoma (10x increased risk), granulomatous disease, enteropathy; Goal: prevent progression by early IgRT and monitoring
Life expectancy is normal without treatment

questions.explanation: CVID complications: (1) Bronchiectasis - from recurrent infections, present in this patient, irreversible; (2) Autoimmunity (25%) - cytopenias (ITP, AIHA), thyroiditis; (3) Lymphoma - 10x increased risk; (4) Granulomatous-lymphocytic interstitial lung disease (GLILD); (5) GI disease - chronic diarrhea, IBD-like, nodular lymphoid hyperplasia. Average diagnostic delay is 5-7 years, during which irreversible damage occurs. Early IgRT reduces infections but established bronchiectasis persists.

whyChain.why: This patient already has bronchiectasis - is it too late?
whyChain.answer: Bronchiectasis is irreversible, but IgRT can prevent progression and reduce exacerbations. She will benefit from treatment but needs ongoing pulmonary monitoring, chest physiotherapy, and possibly prophylactic antibiotics for her established lung disease.

Annual: PFTs (monitor lung function), CT if worsening symptoms. Every 1-3 years: consider CT surveillance for lymphoma (increased risk). Regular monitoring of IgG trough levels, infection frequency, and autoimmune complications.

questions.stem: You are reviewing other primary immunodeficiencies for comparison.
questions.question: What is X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA) and how does it differ from CVID?
questions.hint: Consider the age of presentation and B cell status.

options.text: XLA: BTK gene mutation, ABSENT B cells, presents in infancy after maternal antibodies wane, males only; CVID: B cells PRESENT but dysfunctional, presents later, both sexes

questions.explanation: X-linked agammaglobulinemia (Bruton's): BTK gene mutation → arrested B cell development → ABSENT B cells and immunoglobulins. Presents 6-12 months when maternal IgG wanes. Affects only males. Absent tonsils/lymph nodes. Treatment: lifelong IgRT. CVID differs: B cells present but cannot mature/class-switch, presents later (often adulthood), affects both sexes, mechanism usually unknown.

whyChain.why: How does this patient's family history fit?
whyChain.answer: Her brother who died at age 3 from 'severe infection' could have had XLA or SCID. Her mother's 'low antibodies' suggests CVID (can be familial in ~10%). The pattern suggests inherited antibody deficiency running in the family.

questions.stem: A newborn is found to have T cell lymphopenia on newborn screening.
questions.question: What is SCID and why is it a medical emergency?

options.text: SCID can wait for outpatient evaluation

Severe Combined Immunodeficiency: absent/non-functional T cells (± B/NK), fatal in first 1-2 years without HSCT; presents with opportunistic infections, failure to thrive; EMERGENCY requiring isolation and urgent transplant evaluation

questions.explanation: SCID is a PEDIATRIC EMERGENCY: (1) Absent/non-functional T cells (B and NK variable by subtype); (2) Complete lack of adaptive immunity; (3) Fatal by age 1-2 without treatment; (4) Presents with severe infections (PCP, CMV, disseminated BCG), chronic diarrhea, failure to thrive, persistent thrush; (5) Detected on newborn screening (TREC assay) in many states; (6) Treatment: HSCT (curative), gene therapy for some subtypes. Live vaccines contraindicated. Immediate isolation and immunology referral.

whyChain.why: How is SCID detected early now?
whyChain.answer: Newborn screening using TREC (T-cell receptor excision circles) assay detects low T cells at birth. This allows diagnosis before infections occur and improves HSCT outcomes. Universal screening now recommended.

questions.stem: Integrating all information about this case.
questions.question: What is the complete diagnosis, management, and prognosis for this patient?
questions.hint: Connect the clinical presentation, labs, and treatment plan.

options.text: CVID confirmed by low IgG/IgA/IgM, poor polysaccharide vaccine response, low switched memory B cells; established bronchiectasis from diagnostic delay; treatment: lifelong IgRT + prophylactic antibiotics + pulmonary rehab; monitor for lymphoma and autoimmune complications

questions.explanation: Complete synthesis: (1) Diagnosis: CVID - meets all criteria (low IgG + IgA + IgM, impaired polysaccharide response, low switched memory B cells, secondary causes excluded); (2) Complications already present: bronchiectasis (irreversible), history of autoimmunity (ITP, thyroiditis); (3) Treatment: initiate IgRT (IVIG or SCIG), target trough >700-800 mg/dL, consider azithromycin prophylaxis, chest PT for bronchiectasis; (4) Monitoring: annual PFTs, IgG troughs, infection diary, lymphoma surveillance; (5) Family counseling: mother should be evaluated, genetic counseling offered.

whyChain.why: What about her splenectomy - does that change things?
whyChain.answer: Splenectomy for ITP (an autoimmune complication of CVID) adds risk of encapsulated organism infection. She needs pneumococcal, meningococcal, and Hib vaccination (though response may be poor) and should have emergency antibiotics available. The splenectomy was treating a symptom of her underlying CVID.
